stem:
Consider the following pseudocode:
\[ \text{count} = 0 \]
\[ \textbf{for } i = 1 \textbf{ to } [[n]]: \]
\[ \quad \textbf{for } j = 1 \textbf{ to } [[m]]: \]
\[ \quad \quad \text{count} = \text{count} + 1 \]
How many times does \( \text{count} = \text{count} + 1 \) execute in total?
solution: The inner loop runs [[m]] times for each of the [[n]] outer iterations, and the outer loop's trip count does not depend on \( i \), so the total is \( \sum_{i=1}^{[[n]]} [[m]] = [[n]]\cdot[[m]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This adds the two loop bounds instead of multiplying the trip counts.
This adds an extra outer pass to the correct product.
This removes one inner pass from the correct product.
This assumes both loops run [[n]] times instead of using the actual inner bound [[m]].
This mimics a strict count of pairs with distinct indices, off by not matching either loop bound.
This adds an unrelated adjustment of [[n]] to the correct total.

stem:
Consider the following pseudocode:
\[ \text{count} = 0 \]
\[ \textbf{for } i = 1 \textbf{ to } [[n]]: \]
\[ \quad \textbf{for } j = i \textbf{ to } [[n]]: \]
\[ \quad \quad \text{count} = \text{count} + 1 \]
How many times does \( \text{count} = \text{count} + 1 \) execute in total?
solution: For each \( i \) the inner loop makes \( [[n]]-i+1 \) passes, so the total is \( \sum_{i=1}^{[[n]]} ([[n]]-i+1) = \frac{[[n]]([[n]]+1)}{2} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is exactly double the correct triangular count.
This uses the strict-inequality triangular formula (excludes the diagonal term \(j=i\)) instead of the inclusive one.
This treats the loops as fully independent, as if the inner loop always ran [[n]] times.
This subtracts an extra [[n]] from the correct triangular formula.
This overshoots the correct total by [[n]].
This undershoots the correct total by 1.

stem:
Consider the following pseudocode:
\[ \text{count} = 0 \]
\[ \textbf{for } i = 1 \textbf{ to } [[n]]: \]
\[ \quad \textbf{for } j = i + 1 \textbf{ to } [[n]]: \]
\[ \quad \quad \text{count} = \text{count} + 1 \]
How many times does \( \text{count} = \text{count} + 1 \) execute in total?
solution: For each \( i \) the inner loop makes \( [[n]]-i \) passes (it starts strictly after \( i \)), so the total is \( \sum_{i=1}^{[[n]]} ([[n]]-i) = \frac{[[n]]([[n]]-1)}{2} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the inclusive triangular formula (as if \(j\) started at \(i\), not \(i+1\)) instead of the strict one.
This is exactly double the correct strict-triangular count.
This treats the loops as fully independent, as if the inner loop always ran [[n]] times.
This is exactly double the inclusive triangular formula.
This overshoots the correct total by [[n]].
This overshoots the correct total by 1.

stem:
Consider the following pseudocode:
\[ \text{count} = 0 \]
\[ \textbf{for } i = 1 \textbf{ to } [[n]]: \]
\[ \quad \textbf{for } j = 1 \textbf{ to } [[c]]\cdot i: \]
\[ \quad \quad \text{count} = \text{count} + 1 \]
How many times does \( \text{count} = \text{count} + 1 \) execute in total?
solution: The inner loop makes \( [[c]]\cdot i \) passes for each \( i \), so the total is \( \sum_{i=1}^{[[n]]} [[c]]\cdot i = [[c]]\cdot\frac{[[n]]([[n]]+1)}{2} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This treats the inner bound as always \([[c]]\cdot[[n]]\) instead of growing with \(i\).
This uses the strict-triangular formula for \(i\) instead of the inclusive cumulative sum.
This forgets to scale the triangular sum by [[c]].
This only counts a single pass through the outer loop's scaling factor.
This adds an unrelated adjustment of [[c]] to the correct total.
This subtracts an unrelated adjustment of [[n]] from the correct total.

stem:
Consider the following pseudocode:
\[ \text{count} = 0 \]
\[ \textbf{for } i = 1 \textbf{ to } [[n]]: \]
\[ \quad \textbf{for } j = 1 \textbf{ to } [[m]]: \]
\[ \quad \quad \textbf{for } k = 1 \textbf{ to } [[p]]: \]
\[ \quad \quad \quad \text{count} = \text{count} + 1 \]
How many times does \( \text{count} = \text{count} + 1 \) execute in total?
solution: Each of the three independent loops contributes a factor to the total, so the innermost line runs \( [[n]]\cdot[[m]]\cdot[[p]] = [[answer]] \) times.
distractor_rationales: This adds the three loop bounds instead of multiplying the trip counts.
This sums pairwise products instead of taking the single triple product.
This subtracts an unrelated adjustment of [[n]] from the correct triple product.
This assumes all three loops run [[n]] times instead of using the actual bounds.
This adds an unrelated adjustment of [[m]] to the correct total.
This subtracts an unrelated adjustment of [[p]] from the correct total.

stem:
Consider the following pseudocode:
\[ \text{count} = 0 \]
\[ \textbf{for } i = 1 \textbf{ to } [[n]] \textbf{ step } 2: \]
\[ \quad \textbf{for } j = 1 \textbf{ to } [[m]]: \]
\[ \quad \quad \text{count} = \text{count} + 1 \]
(The outer loop takes the values \( 1, 3, 5, \dots \) up to at most [[n]].) How many times does \( \text{count} = \text{count} + 1 \) execute in total?
solution: The outer loop takes \( \lfloor([[n]]-1)/2\rfloor + 1 = [[trips]] \) values (\(1,3,5,\dots\)), and the inner loop always runs [[m]] times, so the total is \( [[trips]]\cdot[[m]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This ignores the step of 2 and assumes the outer loop runs [[n]] times.
This overcounts the outer trip count by one extra inner pass.
This undercounts the outer trip count by one inner pass.
This uses \(\lfloor [[n]]/2 \rfloor\) instead of the correct \(\lfloor([[n]]-1)/2\rfloor + 1\) outer trip count.
This adds an unrelated adjustment of the outer trip count to the correct total.
This undershoots the correct total by 1.
